experiments.priority: Guided cold-start that seeds a live dashboard and un-bricks the NLP

variants.name: Conversational Intent Picker (drop-in WelcomeThemePicker replacement)
variants.approach: The most surgical, lowest-risk variant: replace the body of the existing first-run gate without touching its plumbing. Keep the exact `showWelcome` state, the `brandNewAccount` guard (Dashboard.tsx:306-308), and the `<WelcomeThemePicker .../>` render slot (Dashboard.tsx:1382) so the change stays strictly additive and skippable. Swap the contents of WelcomeThemePicker.tsx (or a sibling `WelcomeSetup.tsx` rendered in the same slot) for a 2-step conversational flow: Step 1 'What are you here for?' -> 3-4 big cards (Uni / Job hunt / Gym / Just exploring), each mapped to a free shipped world id via `findWorld('study'|'job-hunt')` (Gym can reuse `findWorld('calm')` or fall back to a default-stages topic; 'Just exploring' = today's pure-theme path, preserving the current behaviour). Step 2 reuses the existing Dark/Light ThemeCard pair already in the file. On confirm, call a single new `onSetup(worldId, mood)` callback wired in Dashboard that: (a) builds a real topic via `worldToTopic(world, world.name, topics.length)` and `setTopics([topic])` — this alone flips predictTopic from null to useful (taskParser.ts:263) from capture #1; (b) seeds 2-3 example `TopicItem`s with `deadline` set to today/tomorrow/this-week unix-ms (types.ts:28-29) into that topic's first stage so Today/Upcoming/MiniCalendar light up; (c) applies the matching home layout by mapping the world's intent to a `STARTER_TEMPLATES` entry and calling the same fresh-`i` logic as HomeView.applyTemplate (registry.tsx + HomeView.tsx:189) via `setHomeItems`; (d) sets `appearance.mood` exactly as `chooseWelcomeMood` does today and saves `welcomeComplete`. The four opt-in walkthroughs already auto-suppress because `onbDismissed` derives from `topics.length > 0` (Dashboard.tsx:334), so seeding a topic demotes them to optional 'learn more' for free. Strength: minimal blast radius, every API already exists, TypeScript-trivial. This is the safe baseline.

variants.name: Live-Preview Split Builder (see-your-dashboard-fill-in)
variants.approach: A bolder, more ambitious variant that makes the cold-start feel like watching your life assemble itself rather than answering a form. Full-screen overlay (same portal/zIndex pattern as WelcomeThemePicker) but split layout: LEFT is the question column, RIGHT is a live, shrunk-to-scale real render of the home grid + a faux sidebar that updates in real time as the user answers. As soon as they tap 'Uni', the right panel animates in the seeded `study` world: the wallpaper (world.background.url) washes in, a 'Study' topic appears in the mini-sidebar, and Today/Upcoming/MiniCalendar widgets populate with the 2-3 seeded deadlines — so the value (a live dashboard + un-bricked prediction) is shown before commit, not promised. Implementation: a local React state machine holds {intent, mood, accent} and derives a preview by calling `worldToTopic` + the seeded-deadline builder into throwaway state; the right panel reuses the actual widget components (today/upcomingDeadlines/miniCalendar) inside a `transform: scale()` non-interactive wrapper, or a lightweight static mirror if rendering live widgets in-overlay is too heavy. A secondary micro-step lets them tweak accent from the world's `colorBank` (worlds.ts) for ownership. 'Use this setup' commits exactly the same writes as Variant 1 (setTopics with seeded items, setHomeItems from the mapped starter, appearance mood/accent, welcomeComplete). Add an optional inline 'Try capturing something' box that runs the now-live `predictTopic` against their typed text and shows it routing to the seeded topic — a literal demonstration that the NLP is no longer inert. Strength: highest emotional payoff and 'first paint feels like her life' impact; demonstrates the un-bricking instead of just enabling it. Cost: more UI work and care to keep the live preview cheap; still fully self-contained React/TS with zero new backend.

variants.name: Zero-Question Smart Seed + First-Capture Teach (NLP-first, ask-nothing)
variants.approach: The contrarian variant: question whether ANY questions are the right cold-start. Instead of an intent quiz, seed a sensible default the instant the account is brand-new and move the 'personalisation' into the first real capture, which is where the un-bricked NLP becomes the hero. On brand-new detection (reuse `brandNewAccount`), skip the picker entirely and auto-seed a single neutral but real topic (default stages via `worldToTopic` of the `default`/`study` world) plus a 'Just the essentials' home layout from STARTER_TEMPLATES, with 2-3 example deadlines — so the very first paint is already a live dashboard with Today/Upcoming/MiniCalendar populated and predictTopic non-null. Keep ONE tiny, dismissible top-of-home banner: 'Tell Bozz what your life looks like' -> tap reveals the same 2-3 intent chips (Uni/Job hunt/Gym), and choosing one upgrades the seeded topic in place via `worldTopicPatch` (worlds.ts:316, which re-themes + re-lays-out an existing topic without destroying items) and applies the matching world layout/mood — personalisation as an opt-in upgrade, not a gate. Crucially, the first time the user types into Quicks/quick-add, surface a one-time inline coachmark on the live prediction result ('Bozz guessed this goes to <topic> — accept, or pick another') so the moment that teaches the product is the moment the smartest asset visibly works. This makes the NLP un-bricking the literal centrepiece of onboarding rather than a silent side effect. The four walkthroughs stay demoted (topics.length>0 suppresses them). Strength: fastest possible path to a live screen (0 taps to value), respects 'morning in 90 seconds' most literally, and reframes onboarding around the predictTopic capability that round ranking flagged as the wasted asset. Risk: a wrong default seed feels less 'hers' than an explicit pick, mitigated by the in-place `worldTopicPatch` upgrade path and the fact that nothing seeded is destructive or un-undoable.

votes.why:
As Maya, a first-year student, the thing that would actually make me trust a new app in the first 30 seconds is SEEING my dashboard come alive, not being told it will. The split builder does that: I tap "Uni" and the right panel immediately washes in a wallpaper, drops a Study topic in the sidebar, and lights up Today/Upcoming/MiniCalendar with real-looking deadlines. That "oh, this is mine already" feeling is what gets me past the empty-screen drop-off that kills most productivity apps for me. A form (Variant 1) is fine and safe, but it feels like homework, ask, ask, then maybe something happens, and I have no proof it worked until after I commit. The whole point here is "your morning in 90 seconds," and a preview sells that promise visually before I have to believe it.

The detail that tips it over the zero-question version (Variant 3) is the inline "try capturing something" box that runs the now-live predictTopic against my own typed text and shows it routing to the seeded topic. I confirmed predictTopic literally returns null until topics.length > 0 (taskParser.ts:263), so the smartest part of the app is genuinely dead on a cold account. Variant 3 hides that reveal behind my first real Quicks capture later, which I might not even do on day one, so the un-bricking stays invisible at the exact moment I'm deciding whether to keep the app. Variant 2 makes the NLP working a thing I watch happen with my own words during setup, which is far more convincing than a default seed I didn't choose (Variant 3's risk: it doesn't feel like mine), and far more convincing than Variant 1 which enables prediction silently without ever showing it.

Feasibility-wise I'm satisfied it's real, not a fantasy: it commits the exact same writes as the safe baseline (setTopics with seeded items, setHomeItems via the same fresh-i logic as HomeView.applyTemplate at HomeView.tsx:189-200, appearance mood/accent, welcomeComplete), reuses the existing portal/zIndex pattern from WelcomeThemePicker, and the worldToTopic/findWorld/STARTER_TEMPLATES helpers all already exist in worlds.ts and registry.tsx. The only added cost is a scaled non-interactive preview panel, and the variant already hedges with a "lightweight static mirror" fallback if live widgets are too heavy, so the bold version degrades gracefully without risking a TypeScript-green, self-contained change. For dogfood v2's mandate to favour bold, high-payoff changes over timid polish, this is the one that both moves me emotionally and proves the un-bricked prediction is the hero.

votes.preferred: Zero-Question Smart Seed + First-Capture Teach (NLP-first, ask-nothing)
votes.why:
As Tom the freelancer, my time IS my money and "your morning in 90 seconds" is the whole reason I'd try this. The other two variants both make me stop and answer a quiz before I see anything useful. I don't want to fill in a form to find out if an app is worth my time. The first thing I want is to LAND on a working screen and judge it in two seconds.

I checked the code and the contrarian variant's two scariest claims are both true, which removes my objections: predictTopic literally `return null` when topics.length === 0 (taskParser.ts:263), so seeding one default topic on brand-new detection genuinely un-bricks the NLP from paint #1; and worldTopicPatch (worlds.ts:316) only patches color/icon/pageBg/widgetLayout and never touches `items`, so the in-place "upgrade" when I later tap Uni/Job hunt/Gym is provably non-destructive. That means the "wrong default feels less mine" risk is fully mitigated: nothing I'd lose is at stake, and I can re-theme without re-doing work.

The deciding factor is the first-capture coachmark. The single most valuable thing here is that Bozz reads "invoice client Friday" and routes it to the right place. Variant 2 demonstrates that in a sandbox before commit, which is nice theatre, but the contrarian version teaches it on my REAL first capture, at the exact moment I'm testing whether this tool is smart. That's where a freelancer decides to keep an app. Zero gate to value, plus the smartest feature proving itself on my actual data, beats a prettier quiz I have to sit through.

My honest reservation: a freelancer is not a student, and a "study"-flavoured default seed could feel slightly off on first paint. But the one dismissible banner with Uni/Job-hunt/Gym chips fixes that in one tap (and "Job hunt"/"Gym" map cleanly to shipped worlds via findWorld), and the upgrade is non-destructive per the code. So I'd happily live with a neutral default for the 0-tap win. I'd reject Variant 1 (safe but it's still a gate that asks before it gives) and Variant 2 (highest polish, but it spends its budget making me watch a preview instead of getting me to work — and the live-render-in-overlay is the riskiest to keep cheap). Variant 3 respects my time most, fixes the real bricked asset, and makes the NLP the hero where it counts.

As Sam (power user), this is the one I'd actually choose, and it best serves the stated goal: "Guided cold-start that seeds a live dashboard AND un-bricks the NLP." It is the only variant where both halves of the goal are made visible at the moment of decision, not merely promised or silently enabled.

Why it beats the other two for me specifically:

1) It shows the un-bricking instead of trusting it. The whole premise is that predictTopic (taskParser.ts:263 returns null until topics.length>0) is a wasted asset. The Split Builder's optional "Try capturing something" box runs the now-live predictTopic against my own typed text and shows it routing to the seeded topic. As a power user, that single demo is what converts me: I see the smartest part of the product work before I commit, rather than discovering it by accident later. Variant 3 also features the NLP, but only AFTER first capture via a coachmark, i.e. it teaches reactively; the Split Builder lets me provoke it on demand during setup, which is what a power user does.

2) "First paint feels like her life" is the actual north-star moment. The right-panel live render (wallpaper washes in, a Study/Job-hunt topic appears, Today/Upcoming/MiniCalendar populate from the 2-3 seeded deadlines) is the literal embodiment of "your morning in 90 seconds." Watching the dashboard assemble itself is a far stronger first impression than a 2-card quiz (Variant 1) or a silent auto-seed (Variant 3), and it earns the one extra screen of attention.

3) It commits the exact same safe writes as the surgical baseline. Per the spec, "Use this setup" performs identical writes to Variant 1 (setTopics with seeded items, setHomeItems from the mapped STARTER_TEMPLATE, appearance mood/accent, welcomeComplete). I verified every API it relies on exists and behaves as claimed: worldToTopic (worlds.ts:296) builds real stages + a fresh widgetLayout; applyTemplate (HomeView.tsx:189) regenerates fresh i keys so React keys never collide; onbDismissed derives from topics.length>0 (Dashboard.tsx:334) so the four walkthroughs auto-demote for free; the showWelcome/brandNewAccount/render-slot plumbing (Dashboard.tsx:306-308, 1382) is untouched and the change stays skippable. So the blast radius on commit is the same as the safe baseline; the added cost is purely additive preview UI, and the spec already provides the escape hatch (a lightweight static mirror) if live widgets in-overlay are too heavy. That keeps it self-contained React/TS with zero backend, satisfying the hard constraints.

Why not the others. Variant 1 (Conversational Intent Picker) is the correct safe baseline and I'd accept it, but it under-delivers on the goal: it ENABLES the live dashboard and un-bricks the NLP without ever showing either, so the payoff lands offscreen. It also has a small real wart I confirmed: it maps "Gym" to findWorld('calm'), but 'calm' is free:false (worlds.ts:210), a Plus-gated world, so that branch must fall back to default stages anyway, undercutting its "every API already exists, trivially" pitch. Variant 3 (Zero-Question Smart Seed) is the most intellectually honest about "morning in 90 seconds" and 0 taps to value, and its worldTopicPatch upgrade path (worlds.ts:316) is genuinely non-destructive, but for me the cost is ownership: a guessed default feels generic, and dogfood loop v2 is explicitly AGGRESSIVE/bold, not minimal. A power user wants to steer the cold-start, not be handed a neutral seed and a dismissible banner. The Split Builder gives me agency (intent + accent from the world colorBank) AND speed AND a visible proof that the NLP works, which is the most complete answer to this round's goal.

experiments.priority: Close the capture-to-Today gap and make brain-dump the hero, so capture finally pays off in the morning view

variants.name: Variant A — Close the last leak: make Today consume untriaged Quicks + one-tap actions (surgical, highest-trust, smallest diff)
variants.approach:
PREMISE: the only surviving 'capture vanished' path is a dated Quick that hasn't been triaged into a topic yet — deadlineEntries() (util.ts) ignores the inbox entirely, so 'submit essay today' captured via Ctrl+B sits in Quicks and never reaches the morning view. This variant closes that leak at the data layer and makes every row in Today actionable, with almost no new UI surface.

CHANGES:
1) Widen the Today data source. Add `inbox: InboxItem[]` (and `onInboxChange`/`onTriageInbox`) to WidgetCtx (context.ts) and thread it through Dashboard's two ctx={{...}} blocks (already has `inbox` state, lines ~88/1420). In util.ts, extend deadlineEntries(ctx) to also emit entries for inbox items where `item.deadline != null`, tagged with a synthetic section `'inbox'` and accent = predictTopic(text)?.color ?? t.borderStrong, carrying a flag `source:'inbox'`. Today's priorities() then automatically buckets them overdue/today/week with zero changes to TodayWidget's bucketing.
2) Distinguish inbox priorities visually: in PrioritiesSection render an inbox-sourced row with a small Inbox icon + 'in Quicks' tag instead of the topic flag, so the user understands it isn't filed yet — and clicking it deep-links to the Quicks triage row (setActiveSection('inbox')) instead of a topic.
3) One-tap actions on every Today row. For topic-sourced priorities, add a trailing check button that calls onAdvanceStage to the done stage (complete) — reusing the existing onAdvanceStage in ctx. For inbox-sourced priorities, add a one-tap 'File' button that triages the Quick into its predicted topic (reuse Dashboard.addTopicItem + remove from inbox), so the morning view becomes the place you ACT, not just read.
4) Add one-tap 'Add to today' on Quicks rows (InboxView InboxRow) and on topic task rows (TopicTodosWidget): a small pill that pushes the item id into dailyPlan[todayKey] via ctx.onDailyPlanChange. This honors the manual-plan path for people who want it, but it is now optional sugar, not the only road in.

WHY DIFFERENT: this is the minimal, conservative fix — it changes data plumbing + adds affordances, ships no new screen, and is the safest TypeScript-green change. It treats Today as the single source of truth and makes the planner truly optional. Best first PR. Risk: low. Net new files: 0 (edits to util.ts, context.ts, TodayWidget.tsx, InboxView.tsx, TopicTodosWidget.tsx, Dashboard.tsx).

variants.name: Variant B — The 'Add a deadline' quick object + auto Uni topic (kills the cold-start wall for the student's primary object)
variants.approach:
PREMISE: the student's atomic unit is 'assignment with a due date', but today you cannot create one without first building a topic with stages in Settings. That wall is why capture never pays off for a first-run user: they have nothing dated to show in Today. This variant introduces a friction-free dated-task creator reachable from every empty/idle surface and auto-scaffolds a destination so the loop completes on the very first try.

CHANGES:
1) New component src/components/AddDeadlineModal.tsx (a small, focused dialog, NOT the ramble modal): three fields — title (autofocus), due date (reuse shared/DatePicker, which already exists and is used in InboxView/QuickAddModal), and an optional course/topic ChoicePicker (reuse shared/ChoicePicker) whose options are existing topics PLUS a default '+ New course' that, on submit, lazily creates a topic. It also accepts an optional time-of-day that maps to ListItem.dueMin (already supported by dueTimestamp in util.ts).
2) Auto-create destination: if no topic is chosen, create/find an auto 'Uni' topic with a sensible default 3-stage pipeline (To do / Doing / Done) via a new Dashboard helper ensureUniTopic() that appends to topics with nextId()/default stages (mirror the existing topic shape; do NOT touch schema — this is just local Topic state already persisted to the 'topics' storage key). Then call existing addTopicItem(topicId, title, deadline). Because addTopicItem files into a real topic with a real deadline, the item appears in Today's Priorities INSTANTLY (closing the loop) and in the calendar/mini-calendar via topicDeadlineEvents.
3) Reachability (the 'scaffolding' the prompt asks for): wire a '+ Add a deadline' affordance into (a) the Today widget header (a small + button next to the date, opening the modal via a ctx callback onAddDeadline), (b) the empty Calendar/mini-calendar empty state, and (c) the QuickAdd flow as a secondary 'just a dated task' shortcut. All three call the same Dashboard-level openAddDeadline state.
4) Empty-state copy: when a user has zero dated items, Today's PrioritiesSection empty state changes from 'Capture a task ->' to 'Add your first deadline ->' opening this modal, so the very first action a new user can take produces a visible Today item.

WHY DIFFERENT: Variant A fixes plumbing; Variant B attacks the COLD START — it makes the primary object creatable in ~10 seconds with no topic ceremony, and guarantees the new-user demo (the 90-second morning) has something in it. It is the highest-leverage move for activation/retention. Risk: medium (introduces topic auto-creation; must reuse exact existing Topic/stage shape to stay schema-safe and TS-green). Net new files: 1 (AddDeadlineModal.tsx) + edits to Dashboard.tsx, TodayWidget.tsx, calendar/mini-calendar empty states.

variants.name: Variant C — 'Brain Dump' full-screen hero: promote the ramble flow into the landing-GIF differentiator (boldest, demo-defining)
variants.approach:
PREMISE: the offline-NLP parser (parseVoiceTasks) is the one thing Notion/Things/Sunsama cannot ship without a backend, and it is currently buried in a small centered QuickAddModal plus a redundant legacy VoiceTaskReview panel. This variant makes brain-dump the literal hero: a full-screen capture canvas that live-previews a messy paragraph as editable, dated, topic-coloured chips with one 'File all' — the 15-second landing demo.

CHANGES:
1) New full-screen section src/components/sections/BrainDumpView.tsx (its own SectionId 'brainDump', added to Dashboard's builtInSectionIds + a prominent entry point). Left/top: one big always-focused textarea + the existing VoiceButton (mic). As the user types or speaks, debounce-call parseVoiceTasks(text, topics) (the SAME parser QuickAddModal already uses) and render the result as a responsive grid of chips. Reuse QuickAddModal's TaskCard (extract it to a shared src/components/shared/TaskCard.tsx so both surfaces share one component — this also lets us DELETE the now-redundant VoiceTaskReview.tsx, removing duplicate logic). Each chip shows text (inline-editable), a topic ChoicePicker (topic-coloured), and a DatePicker; parser-predicted topic/deadline are pre-filled and visibly highlighted as 'AI-detected'.
2) Live, paragraph-to-chips theatre: show the raw paragraph at top with the matched date phrases + verb splits subtly underlined in the chip accent colour, so the user SEES the offline NLP working (this is the GIF moment). Counts update live: 'Found 4 tasks · 2 dated · 3 routed to topics'.
3) One 'File all' (primary) composes the existing paths exactly like QuickAddModal.addAll(): chips with a topic -> addTopicItem(topicId, text, deadline) (so dated ones land in Today's Priorities immediately); chips left on 'Quicks' -> addToInbox. Secondary 'Send all to Quicks' = triage later. On success, route the user straight to Home so they watch the items they just dumped appear in the Today widget — capture visibly paying off in the morning view, in one screen transition.
4) Make it the hero entry point: replace/augment the small QuickAdd trigger so the primary capture CTA (and the onboarding 'try it' step) opens Brain Dump full-screen; keep the lightweight Ctrl+B QuickCapture for one-liners. Auto-create a 'Uni' fallback topic (shared helper, same as Variant B) so 'File all' never has nowhere to put a dated chip on a fresh account.

WHY DIFFERENT: Variants A/B are about correctness and cold-start; Variant C is the bold PRODUCT-NARRATIVE move — it turns an existing modal into the signature, demoable surface, consolidates two parser UIs into one shared component (net code REDUCTION via deleting VoiceTaskReview), and choreographs the capture->Today payoff as a visible transition. Highest ambition + best for the landing GIF and growth story. Risk: medium-high (new full section + onboarding wiring + component extraction), but it leans entirely on already-shipped parser/handlers. Net new files: 2 (BrainDumpView.tsx, shared/TaskCard.tsx) − 1 deleted (VoiceTaskReview.tsx); edits to Dashboard.tsx, registry/onboarding entry points.

votes.preferred: Variant A — Close the last leak: make Today consume untriaged Quicks + one-tap actions (surgical, highest-trust, smallest diff)
votes.why: As Maya (first-year, terrified of missing a deadline) I picked A because it fixes the exact thing that makes me distrust this app. I verified the bug is real: InboxItem already carries a deadline (types.ts line 160) and the parser even stamps one on when I Ctrl+B "submit essay today," but deadlineEntries() in util.ts (lines 11-29) only loops ctx.topics and never touches the inbox. So my dated Quick lands in Quicks and is invisible in the Today widget forever unless I do a separate triage chore I don't know I have to do. That is the "capture vanished" moment, and it happens on the very first thing I capture. A closes it at the data layer so the morning view finally tells the truth, which is the entire promise ("your morning in 90 seconds"). It also adds one-tap File/complete so Today becomes where I act, not just read, and the "Add to today" pill makes the manual planner optional sugar instead of the only road in.\n\nI rejected B and C even though they're flashier. B (Add-a-deadline modal + auto-Uni topic) attacks cold start, but cold start is not my pain: I CAN already capture in ten seconds via Ctrl+B, the problem is it doesn't show up. B leaves the deadlineEntries leak completely unfixed, so a dated Quick I capture by voice/typing still vanishes; it only helps if I happen to use the new modal, and it silently auto-creates topics I didn't ask for, which feels like the app making a mess in my space. C (full-screen Brain Dump) is the boldest demo, but for ME it's largely re-skinning what QuickAddModal already does: live paragraph-to-chips, parseVoiceTasks, topic ChoicePicker, DatePicker, and "Add all" all exist there today (QuickAddModal.tsx lines 318-334, 186-217). C is a growth/landing-GIF move, not a fix for my trust, and its addAll still files chips into topics, so an untriaged dated chip left on "Quicks" STILL won't reach Today: the underlying leak survives. A is the only variant that makes capture actually pay off in the morning view, it's the lowest-risk and most TypeScript-safe (edits to util.ts/context.ts/TodayWidget/InboxView/Dashboard, zero new files), and it's the foundation B and C would both need anyway. Fix the leak first; make it pretty later. votes.preferred: Variant A — Close the last leak: make Today consume untriaged Quicks + one-tap actions (surgical, highest-trust, smallest diff)
votes.why:
As Tom, a freelancer who already lives in this app daily, Variant A fixes the exact thing that actually burns me — and I verified the bug is real in the code, not hypothetical. deadlineEntries() in src/components/widgets/util.ts (lines 11-29) only walks ctx.topics and never touches the inbox, yet InboxItem (src/lib/types.ts:153) carries a deadline. So when I Ctrl+B "send Acme invoice today" mid-call and it lands in Quicks untriaged, my Today widget silently omits it. That is a trust-breaking leak: capture looks like it worked, then the morning view lies to me. Variant A closes it at the data layer (thread inbox into WidgetCtx, extend deadlineEntries to emit inbox-sourced entries) so priorities() buckets them with zero changes to the bucketing logic, and it makes Today the single source of truth. The one-tap File button is cheap to build because the machinery already exists — predictTopic and addTopicItem are both live (src/components/Dashboard.tsx:688/680, src/lib/taskParser.ts:262) and InboxView already re-predicts per row. So the morning view becomes where I ACT (file or complete in one tap), not just read. Crucially, the manual dailyPlan path becomes optional sugar instead of the only road in — that is the real win for someone who captures fast and never wants to babysit a planner.

Why not B or C: both are aimed at the student cold-start and the new-user demo / landing GIF, which is not my problem — I am an existing user with topics already. B's auto-create 'Uni' topic is literally wrong for a freelancer (I would get a junk topic), and it carries medium risk because it mutates topic state with auto-scaffolding. C is the boldest but it is a growth/narrative play (new full-screen section + component extraction + onboarding rewiring, medium-high risk) that does not, by itself, fix the silent drop of my dated Quick. A is the smallest diff, the safest TypeScript-green change (0 new files), and the highest-trust fix — it makes capture finally pay off in the morning for people who are already relying on it. It is the right first PR; B or C can follow once the leak is sealed.

votes.preferred: Variant A — Close the last leak: make Today consume untriaged Quicks + one-tap actions
votes.why:
As Sam (power user), the daily pain isn't cold-start or a missing demo screen — it's silent data loss. I verified the leak is exactly as A states: priorities() in TodayWidget calls deadlineEntries() in util.ts, which iterates ONLY ctx.topics and never touches the inbox. So a Ctrl+B "submit essay today" sits in Quicks and never reaches the morning view. That's the one bug that destroys trust in a capture tool, and it's the gap I feel every morning. A fixes it at the data layer with almost no new surface to learn.

A is also the lowest-risk, highest-trust diff and it reuses what's already there: InboxItem already carries `deadline` and `suggestedTopicId`, the inbox deep-link target already exists (PrioritiesSection's empty state already calls setActiveSection('inbox') at TodayWidget.tsx:124), and onAdvanceStage/addTopicItem/addToInbox/onDailyPlanChange/dailyPlan are all already on WidgetCtx (context.ts:17-25). So the only genuinely new plumbing is `inbox` + a triage callback threaded through Dashboard's existing ctx blocks. The one-tap complete/File actions turn Today from a read-only list into the place I act — which is what I want as someone who hates ceremony. Crucially, A makes the manual daily-planner OPTIONAL rather than the only road into Today, which matches how a power user actually captures (fast, trust it, move on).

Why not B: it attacks cold-start (auto-creating a 'Uni' topic with default stages), which helps a brand-new user but does nothing for me — I already have topics, and auto-scaffolding a course feels presumptuous and clutters my real topic list. Medium risk for a problem I don't have.

Why not C: it's the boldest and best for the landing GIF, but it's a growth/marketing bet, not a fix for the capture-to-Today gap I live with. It builds a whole new full-screen section + onboarding rewiring I'd have to adopt, and its headline selling point ('net code reduction by deleting VoiceTaskReview, consolidating two parser UIs') is overstated: I grepped and VoiceTaskReview.tsx has ZERO import sites — it's already dead code, so deleting it isn't real consolidation. C's actual cost (new section, live-parse theatre, onboarding wiring) stays, while its payoff is demo narrative rather than closing the leak.

changes.title: Ship the timetable front-door: inline 'Paste your timetable' card on the empty Calendar (Variant A)
changes.approach: Wire the existing-but-unreachable .ics pipeline to a UI. Add an `onAddFeed?: (feed: CalendarFeed) => void` prop to CalendarView (Dashboard passes `feed => setCalendarFeeds(prev => [...prev, feed])`, which already triggers refreshFeeds via the effect at Dashboard.tsx:731 and flows into feedEvents/todayEvents). In CalendarView, render an inline empty-state card ABOVE the grid whenever `feedEvents.length === 0 && calendarConnections.length === 0` (also reachable any time via a small 'Add timetable' ghost button in the SectionHeader `right` slot, next to 'today'). The card holds: one text input for a webcal/https .ics URL, an auto-name field (defaulted from the URL host), a ColorBankPicker (reuse the existing `colorBank` prop + ColorBankPicker already imported), and a Save button. On save, normalise `webcal://` -> `https://` and build `{ id: <random36>, label, url }` (CalendarFeed shape at types.ts:261) and call onAddFeed. Add a one-line clipboard paste-detect: on the input's `onPaste` (and an onFocus navigator.clipboard.readText guarded in try/catch), if the pasted text matches `/^(webcal:|https?:).*\.ics($|\?)/i` or starts with `webcal://`, auto-fill the URL and pre-fill the name from the host so it feels magic. No backend, no parser change needed — parseICal/fetchFeed already handle fetch+expand. Optionally export a tiny `normalizeFeedUrl(url)` helper from ical.ts for the webcal->https swap so both Dashboard and the card share it.
changes.risk: Low — purely additive React wiring onto an existing, tested pipeline (setCalendarFeeds already persists via Dashboard.tsx:382 and refreshes via :731). No auth/secrets. Main edge: clipboard read can throw on web without permission, so it must be try/catch'd and never block manual entry. CORS is already handled by platformFetch (Tauri).

changes.title: Unclip the calendar time grid so early lectures and late shifts render
changes.approach: CalendarView hardcodes GRID_START_HOUR=6 / GRID_END_HOUR=22 (lines 33-34), clipping a 08:00 imported timetable's earlier slots and any event after 22:00 (it computes topPx off GRID_START_HOUR at 333 and the now-line at 229). Make the grid auto-fit the rendered week/day: compute dynamic start/end hours inside TimeGrid from the min start and max end across all timed events in `columns` (fall back to the current 6-22 window when empty, and always clamp to a sensible 0-24 with at least the 6-22 span so a sparse day still looks normal). Pass the computed bounds down to the hour-label loop, hour-lines, now-line %, and event topPx/height math (replace every literal GRID_START_HOUR/GRID_END_HOUR reference, ~lines 213-334). Keep HOUR_PX constant. This guarantees a freshly-pasted timetable shows its 9am lecture and 6pm seminar in full.
changes.risk: Low — self-contained to one component's render math. Risk is an off-by-one in the new min/max hour rounding; mitigated by flooring start-hour and ceiling end-hour and keeping the 6-22 minimum window.

changes.title: Replace the theme-only first-run with a Live-Preview Split Builder that seeds a real topic + deadlines and un-bricks predictTopic
changes.approach: Rebuild the brand-new-account first-run (gated by the existing `brandNewAccount` + showWelcome path at Dashboard.tsx:306-308 / render at :1382) as a split-screen: left = a 1-2 question intent picker ('What are you here for?' -> Uni / Job hunt / Both, plus the existing Dark/Light choice retained as a compact toggle so chooseWelcomeMood at :340 still fires); right = a LIVE mini-preview of the home filling in. On confirm: (a) build a real seeded topic with the EXISTING machinery — `worldToTopic(findWorld('study'|'job-hunt'), name, order)` (worlds.ts:296; both templates are free and ship full pages+stages) and push it into setTopics; (b) seed 2-3 example deadline items onto that topic via addTopicItem (Dashboard.tsx:673) with deadlines spread across today/this-week so Today, Upcoming and MiniCalendar light up immediately; (c) apply the matching world appearance via applyWorld (worlds.ts:254) for mood/colorBank/font. Keep it fully skippable ('Skip, I'll set up later' -> falls back to today's blank+theme path). Because a topic now exists from capture #1, predictTopic (taskParser.ts:262, which hard-returns null when topics.length===0) becomes useful instantly. Demote the four Onboarding walkthroughs to optional 'Learn more' (they already render conditionally at Dashboard.tsx:1384 and are re-runnable from Settings via replayWalkthroughs).
changes.risk: Medium — touches first-run state orchestration. Must preserve the existing welcomeComplete save (:343) and the brandNewAccount guard so returning users never see it, and seeded items must be clearly example data (prefix/label) so they're easy to delete. No schema/auth touched: topics+deadlines persist through the existing setTopics/save path. Keep the seed idempotent (only when topics.length===0).

changes.title: Make Today auto-include tasks due today/overdue instead of only the manual daily plan (Variant A core)
changes.approach: Close the capture-to-Today data leak. TasksSection (TodayWidget.tsx:271) reads STRICTLY from `dailyPlan[todayKey]`, so a task triaged with a deadline of today never appears, even though the widget already computes `priorities()` from deadlineEntries (lines 68-84) independently. Fix: the widget already shows priorities as the top block, so make the 'My plan' block additive rather than the source of truth — relabel/merge so that any item in `priorities()` with bucket 'today' or 'overdue' is guaranteed visible in Today, and have TasksSection UNION the dailyPlan items with the due-today/overdue items from priorities() (dedupe by `${topic.id}-${item.id}`), rather than dailyPlan alone. Items pulled from priorities keep the same stage-pill/advance UI (onAdvanceStage is already in ctx). Demote the manual planner to a clearly-optional 'plan your day' affordance (the empty-state link to dailyPlanner already exists at :296). Net effect: capture -> triage with today's deadline -> appears in Today with zero manual drag.
changes.risk: Low-Medium — contained to one widget. Risk is double-rendering an item in both Priorities and My-plan blocks; mitigated by deduping and/or having Priorities own due-today while My-plan shows only extra planned-but-not-due items. No data writes, read-only aggregation.

changes.title: One-tap 'Add to Today' on Quicks triage and topic tasks
changes.approach: Add a friction-free path from capture to the morning view. In InboxView's row (InboxRow, where onAssign(dest, deadline) is wired at InboxView.tsx:63/120), add a small 'Add to today' action that, on assign, ALSO appends the new item's id to `dailyPlan[todayKey]`. Thread a new optional callback through InboxView props -> Dashboard. In Dashboard, the assign handler already creates the topic item (addTopicItem / the inline onAddTopicItem); extend the onAssign passed to InboxView so when 'add to today' is chosen it also calls setDailyPlan to push that item id under the local-midnight key (mirrors how DailyPlannerView mutates dailyPlan). Since the previous change already makes due-today items appear automatically, this button primarily covers items WITHOUT a today deadline that the user still wants surfaced now. Keep the existing 'Accept all predicted' bulk flow (InboxView.tsx:169) untouched.
changes.risk: Low — additive callback plumbing; dailyPlan already exists in state (Dashboard.tsx:124) and is persisted. Must generate/track the new item id consistently (addTopicItem uses nextId()); to keep it simple, surface the 'add to today' as a post-assign toast/secondary action keyed off the just-created id, or have addTopicItem return the id.

changes.title: Full-screen 'Brain Dump' hero that live-previews messy text as dated, topic-coloured chips with 'File all' (the offline-NLP differentiator)
changes.approach: Promote the existing parser into a landing-GIF-worthy surface by composing parts that already exist. New BrainDump.tsx: a large textarea where the user pastes/rambles a paragraph; on each change, run the existing parser (the same parseVoiceTasks/predictTopic + extractDeadline machinery in taskParser.ts that VoiceTaskReview already consumes) to live-render each detected task as an editable chip showing predicted topic colour + extracted deadline (reuse the chip/topic-picker UX from VoiceTaskReview.tsx, which already does text edit + TopicChip + deadline display). A single 'File all' button routes each chip through the existing addToInbox (Dashboard.tsx:687) or addTopicItem path — when a chip has a confident predicted topic it files straight in, otherwise it lands in Quicks. Trigger it from a header/quick-add entry point and from the Today/Calendar empty states so it's the hero action for a new user. This is the visible payoff of the offline NLP and pairs the capture loop with the new Today auto-include.
changes.risk: Medium — new component, but built entirely from existing parser + review UI primitives (no new parsing logic, no backend). Risk is re-parsing on every keystroke feeling janky on long input; mitigate by debouncing the parse. Keep it skippable and non-blocking; reuse ParsedTask types so it stays TypeScript-green.

plan.summary:
Seven grounded, bold-but-safe changes for this round, all verified against real files and respecting the no-auth/payments/secrets/CI constraint.

Priority 1 (timetable front-door, Variant A): The full .ics pipeline already exists (parseICal/fetchFeed in lib/ical.ts, feedEvents at Dashboard.tsx:741, todayEvents at :753) but setCalendarFeeds is only ever called from storage hydration (:199) — never from UI. The fix is an inline empty-state card in CalendarView with a webcal/.ics URL field + clipboard paste-detect that calls a new onAddFeed -> setCalendarFeeds; the existing refresh effect (:731) fills Calendar and Today automatically. Paired with unclipping the hardcoded GRID_START_HOUR=6/GRID_END_HOUR=22 (CalendarView.tsx:33-34) so imported early/late events render.

Priority 2 (guided cold-start, Live-Preview Split Builder): Replace the cosmetic WelcomeThemePicker (gated by brandNewAccount at Dashboard.tsx:306) with a split-screen intent picker that seeds a REAL topic via the existing worldToTopic()/findWorld('study'|'job-hunt') machinery (worlds.ts:296, both free) plus 2-3 example deadlines, lighting up Today/Upcoming/MiniCalendar and flipping predictTopic (taskParser.ts:262, returns null at topics.length===0) to useful from capture #1. Walkthroughs demote to optional.

Priority 3 (capture-to-Today gap, Variant A): TodayWidget's TasksSection reads strictly from dailyPlan[todayKey] (:275) while priorities() already computes due-today/overdue from deadlineEntries (:68) — so make Today union them and dedupe, demoting the manual planner. Add one-tap 'Add to today' on Quicks triage (InboxView onAssign). Plus a full-screen Brain Dump hero composing the existing parser + VoiceTaskReview chip UI + addToInbox/addTopicItem for the offline-NLP demo moment.

Key files: src/components/sections/calendar/CalendarView.tsx, src/lib/ical.ts, src/components/Dashboard.tsx, src/components/onboarding/WelcomeThemePicker.tsx, src/lib/worlds.ts, src/components/widgets/TodayWidget.tsx, src/components/sections/InboxView.tsx, src/components/VoiceTaskReview.tsx, src/lib/taskParser.ts.
